Atelectasis: Collapse of a lung or part of a lung, leading to decreased gas exchange.
Cardiomegaly: Enlargement of the heart, which can be caused by various cardiovascular conditions.
Effusion: Pleural effusion is an abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pleural space between the lungs and the chest wall.
Infiltration: Substance denser than air, such as pus, blood, or protein, which lingers within the parenchyma of the lungs.
Mass: A localized, space-occupying lesion within the lung, typically >3 cm in diameter, suggestive of a tumor or granuloma.
Nodule: A small, distinct radiopaque opacity within the lung, typically <3 cm in diameter.
Pneumonia: An infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid or pus.
Pneumothorax: The presence of air or gas in the cavity between the lungs and the chest wall, causing collapse of the lung.
Consolidation: A region of normally compressible lung tissue that has filled with liquid instead of air.
Edema: Pulmonary edema is fluid accumulation in the tissue and air spaces of the lungs, typically due to heart failure.
Emphysema: A condition in which the air sacs of the lungs are damaged and enlarged, causing breathlessness.
Fibrosis: Pulmonary fibrosis involves scarring and thickening of lung tissue over time.
Pleural_Thickening: Thickening of the pleura, the membrane surrounding the lungs, often caused by inflammation or asbestos exposure.
Hernia: Hiatal hernia occurs when the upper part of the stomach pushes through the diaphragm into the chest cavity.
